title: 1 Chronicles 8
subtitle: Benjamin’s Genealogy — Saul’s Line

refs.note: Benjamin’s tribal allotment, which included Jerusalem and key towns along the Judah-Benjamin border.

notes.note: That these families “lived in Jerusalem” connects the ancient genealogy to the post-exilic present. The returned community can trace their residence in the holy city back through centuries. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase “according to their genealogies” (lĕtōlĕdōtām) indicates that the Chronicler is working from written genealogical records, not oral tradition. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Benjamin receives a second, fuller genealogy (expanding the brief entry in 7:6–12). The detail reflects Benjamin’s importance to the post-exilic community: Jerusalem was in Benjamin’s territory, and many returnees were Benjaminites. The genealogy lists clans, heads of families, and settlements — including Aijalon and Gath, indicating territorial claims that extended into the Shephelah.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew tôlĕḏôt (generations) is a structuring device that traces the thread of God's purpose through family lines. From Genesis to Chronicles, genealogies are not mere lists but theological arguments: they demonstrate continuity, establish legitimacy, and show that God's covenant promises pass from generation to generation.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 29–40 — The Gibeon Genealogy Leading to Saul

refs.note: Saul’s introduction in Samuel provides the same ancestry: Kish son of Abiel, a Benjaminite. The Chronicler’s version adds generational depth.
The identical genealogy is repeated at the end of chapter 9, creating an inclusio that frames the transition from genealogy to narrative.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Esh-Baal (“man of Baal”) corresponds to Ish-Bosheth (“man of shame”) in 2 Sam 2:8. The substitution of bōshet for baʿal reflects post-exilic scribal sensitivity to Baal names. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The genealogy narrows to the family of Gibeon, tracing from Jeiel (the “father of Gibeon”) through Ner to Kish to Saul. This is the bridge between the genealogies (ch 1–9) and the narrative (ch 10): the last name in the list is the first character in the story. Saul’s family tree is repeated almost identically in 9:35–44 — the Chronicler frames the narrative with the genealogy, ensuring the reader understands Saul’s identity before witnessing his death.

heb.paragraph: Mišpāḥâ (clan/family) is the intermediate social unit between the household (bayit) and the tribe (šēḇeṭ). In the genealogical sections, clan identity determines inheritance, military obligation, and cultic responsibility. The Chronicler's careful attention to clan structures reflects the post-exilic community's concern for legitimate succession.

notes.note: These genealogical records are not mere ancient census data but the sovereign hand of God tracing His redemptive purposes through specific family lines. Every name preserved here testifies to God's faithfulness in maintaining the covenant lineage that would ultimately produce the Messiah (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38).

God's meticulous preservation of these genealogical records demonstrates His commitment to His covenantal promises. Each generation listed is a link in the chain of redemptive history, connecting the patriarchal promises to their ultimate fulfillment in Christ. God does not forget His Word, even across centuries of human failure.

Believers today are grafted into this same covenant lineage through faith in Christ (Gal 3:29). The God who faithfully preserved His people through these generations is the same God who preserves His church today. No purpose of God can be thwarted (Job 42:2), and no believer truly in Christ will be lost (John 10:28-29).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThese were the descendants of Ehud, who were heads of families of those living in Geba and were deported to Manahath:ESVThese are the sons of Ehud (they were heads of fathers\u2019 houses of the inhabitants of Geba, and they were carried into exile to Manahath):NIVSons were born to Shaharaim in Moab after he had divorced his wives Hushim and Baara.ESVAnd Shaharaim fathered sons in the country of Moab after he had sent away Hushim and Baara his wives.

thread.text: Benjamin’s tribal allotment, which included Jerusalem and key towns along the Judah-Benjamin border.
Saul’s introduction in Samuel provides the same ancestry: Kish son of Abiel, a Benjaminite. The Chronicler’s version adds generational depth.
The identical genealogy is repeated at the end of chapter 9, creating an inclusio that frames the transition from genealogy to narrative.

coaching.tip: Benjamin's genealogy is expanded here because Saul comes from Benjamin. The Chronicler needs to introduce Saul's line before his death in chapter 10 — even though the entire Saul narrative is compressed to a single chapter. Benjamin matters only as David's predecessor.

prayer_prompt: Saul's genealogy is traced just before his fall is narrated. Consider asking God about the gap between heritage and faithfulness — what you've inherited versus what you've lived.
